content: p In 1774, British physician-scientist Charles Blagden received an unusual invitation from a fellow physician  to spend time in a small room that was hotter, he wrote,  than it was formerly thought any living creature could bear.   p   p Many people may have been appalled by this offer, but Blagden was delighted by the opportunity for self-experimentation. He marveled as his own temperature remained at 98  Fahrenheit  approximately 37  Celsius , even as the temperature of the room approached 200 F  about 93 C .  p   p Today, this ability to maintain a stable body temperature called homeothermy is known to exist among myriad species of mammals and birds. But there are also some notable exceptions. The body temperature of the fat-tailed dwarf lemur, for example, can fluctuate by nearly 45 F  25 C  over a single day.  p  p  a href    full article  a   p   p  a href

metadata.author: Hannah Thomasy, Knowable Magazine
